feed.title: Combined summary - Aligning privacy incentives in P2MR

entry.title: Combined summary - Aligning privacy incentives in P2MR
entry.summary: The ongoing discussion in the Bitcoin development community centers on a proposed amendment to BIP360, which involves mandating at least one merkle authentication path in the control block of P2MR transactions. This change is aimed at eliminating depth-zero script trees in P2MR, thereby enforcing a requirement for each P2MR address to support a minimum of two spending conditions. The rationale behind this proposal is to address concerns that depth-zero script trees might undermine privacy and efficiency objectives inherent in Bitcoin scripting by favoring smaller witness sizes and economically motivating developers to opt for these over more secure alternatives like taproot script spends. Depth-zero script trees currently provide an economic incentive because they allow for smaller witness sizes, leading to a preference that could compromise the privacy and security of transactions. By requiring a depth-1 tree, the proposed modification seeks to discourage the use of simpler, less private scripting methods under P2MR, aligning its efficiency with that of P2TR. This would not only support standard uses of P2MR—such as hybrid addresses combining Schnorr and post-quantum cryptography leaves—but also encourage the adoption of best practices, including incentivizing users to implement a post-quantum fallback leaf alongside a single EC Schnorr leaf. Feedback on the proposal has been mixed, with some proponents suggesting that while it addresses certain perverse incentives, it might inadvertently hinder the broader systemic migration necessary for addressing potential future cryptographic risks, such as those posed by quantum computing advances. The cost implications of using P2MR compared to P2TRv2 are significant, particularly for "long-tail" users who utilize single-key spending policies and represent a large majority of transaction inputs. These costs could disincentivize the shift to escape-hatch output types crucial for mitigating cryptographic risks. Despite efforts to promote adoption, historical data suggests that transitions to new address formats are slow even when incentivized. For instance, years after introducing incentives to migrate to P2TR, it still secures only a small fraction of the UTXO-set. This underscores a broader challenge in achieving widespread adoption, irrespective of the specific protocol improvements offered by P2TRv2 over P2TR, which are perceived primarily as speculative enhancements rather than immediate benefits. Looking ahead, the development of quantum-safe address formats and signature schemes, along with proactive development of rescue protocols, are considered critical for preparing Bitcoin for potential future challenges. The choice between P2MR and P2TRv2 may not significantly impact early-stage adaptations but will be crucial for optimizing long-term outcomes. Further optimizations, such as making single-signer Schnorr spending cheaper, are being explored and are expected to be detailed in upcoming communications within the Bitcoin development community.
